title: I built OrgX on the wrong primitive
hook: i built my agent system on the wrong primitive. for months.
hypothesis: a confession/mistake hook plus a 'wrong primitive' reframe earns more founder trust + saves than a feature-list hook does for cold reach
caption:
the moment it clicked was watching a second agent pick up work a first one 'finished.' it had the files. it had the last message. and it still had no idea why any of it existed — what we'd already ruled out, what done even meant, who was allowed to approve the thing. and so i'm like, wait, i've been treating the prompt as the unit this whole time, but the prompt ends and the company doesn't. the goal + the decisions + the quality bar have to live somewhere that isn't a chat window that evaporates the second the context runs out. so orgx became the layer underneath claude / cursor / codex instead of another place to go type — a context pack instead of a transcript dump. i changed my mind about the most basic thing i was building on and honestly everything after it got simpler. still ripping out the old assumptions though

#buildinpublic #aiagents #multiagent #devtools #mcp #agentops #founder #aiengineering

title: The 7-field handoff that replaced my transcript dumps
hook: 7 fields. zero transcripts. that's my whole handoff.
hypothesis: a numbered framework hook + one dense screenshot-able list slide drives saves and shares harder than a personal narrative arc does
caption:
i watched two of my agents 'collaborate' by one of them pasting a 300-message transcript to the other and it was so obviously broken. like the second agent had to reverse-engineer the goal, the decisions, what was already tried, all of it, from a wall of text. so i started writing down what a handoff actually needs to carry and it turned into 7 fields: goal, definition of done, decisions + why, permissions, budget, confidence, next action. that's the pack. structured context instead of 'here's everything we said, you figure out what mattered.' and the part that got me is once the handoff carries the why + the bar, work below the threshold just loops back before it ever burns my review time. the transcript was never the artifact. the compiled context is

#buildinpublic #aiagents #multiagent #agentops #mcp #devtools #aiengineering

title: Your agents don't have a memory problem
hook: your agents don't have a memory problem.
hypothesis: a single contrarian reframe (memory vs continuity) + a comment-bait CTA beats a build-log narrative for cold-reach engagement and shares
caption:
someone in my dms was like 'i just need my agents to remember more' and i sat with that for a second, because i used to think the exact same thing. and so we all keep reaching for the same fix — bigger context window, better retrieval, tighter summaries — and the agent still shows up to the next task not knowing what we already decided or what 'done' even means here. right? and that's when it hit me that remembering the conversation and remembering the work are two completely different problems. memory is 'what did we say.' continuity is 'why does this exist + what's the bar + who approves.' the chat can't hold that, because the chat ends. so orgx keeps it in a work graph underneath the client — decisions, approvals, receipts, the quality bar — and the agents read from that instead of from a transcript. anyway i keep watching people optimize the memory and skip the continuity entirely

#aiagents #buildinpublic #multiagent #agentops #mcp #llm #devtools
